greetings_perdón: Perdón apologizes — for bumping into someone or a small mistake. To get attention politely use disculpe; to squeeze past people, con permiso.
greetings_disculpe: Disculpe (formal) gets someone's attention or asks pardon politely — the flag-the-waiter 'excuse me'. Perdón apologizes; con permiso asks for way.
greetings_con-permiso: Con permiso asks for way — leaving a room or passing through a crowd. Perdón apologizes; disculpe gets attention.
greetings_claro: Claro is the everyday conversational 'of course, sure' — very common as a one-word reply. Por supuesto is a touch more emphatic and formal.
greetings_por-supuesto: Por supuesto is the fuller, slightly more formal 'of course'. Claro is the casual everyday one.
greetings_tú: Tú is the informal 'you' — friends, family, peers — and the default in Spain. Usted is formal and takes third-person verb forms.
greetings_usted: Usted is the formal, respectful 'you' (strangers, elders, officialdom) and takes third-person verbs. Tú is the informal default in Spain.
greetings_este: Este is 'this' for masculine nouns: este libro. Feminine is esta; for unnamed things or ideas use neuter esto.
greetings_esta: Esta is 'this' for feminine nouns: esta casa. Masculine is este; unnamed things or ideas take neuter esto.
greetings_esto: Esto is the neuter 'this' — for unnamed things and ideas (¿qué es esto?). It never sits before a noun; those are este/esta.
greetings_ese: Ese is 'that' for masculine nouns: ese coche. Feminine is esa; unnamed things or ideas take neuter eso.
greetings_esa: Esa is 'that' for feminine nouns: esa mesa. Masculine is ese; unnamed things or ideas take neuter eso.
greetings_eso: Eso is the neuter 'that' — for unnamed things and ideas (eso es verdad). It never sits before a noun; those are ese/esa.
greetings_estos: Estos is 'these' for masculine (or mixed) plurals: estos días. Feminine plural is estas.
greetings_estas: Estas is 'these' for feminine plurals: estas semanas. Masculine or mixed plural is estos.
greetings_esos: Esos is 'those' for masculine (or mixed) plurals: esos zapatos. Feminine plural is esas.
greetings_esas: Esas is 'those' for feminine plurals: esas flores. Masculine or mixed plural is esos.
colors_suave: Suave is smooth-soft: surfaces, skin, sounds, mild flavors. Something squishy that yields when pressed — a pillow, tender meat — is blando.
physical_blando: Blando yields to pressure — soft pillows, tender meat (and figuratively a 'soft', lenient person). Smooth or gentle textures and sounds are suave.
animals_pez: El pez swims — the live animal. Once it's caught and headed for a plate, it becomes pescado.
food_pescado: Pescado is fish as food — on a menu or at the market. The living animal in the water is un pez.
animals_grillo: Grillo chirps — the insect. The sport is el críquet.
sports_críquet: Críquet is the sport. The chirping insect is un grillo.
food_costilla: Same word as the anatomy term — costillas on a menu are ribs to eat.
body_costilla: Same word as the dish — this costilla is the anatomical rib in your chest.
house_ventilador: Ventilador is the machine that moves air. A sports or music fan is un aficionado.
sports_aficionado: Aficionado is an enthusiast — a sports or music fan. The air-moving machine is un ventilador.
family_prima: Prima is specifically a female cousin. A male cousin is primo.
family_primo: Primo is specifically a male cousin. A female cousin is prima.
health_doctor: Doctor is mostly the title you address someone by (¡doctor!). Naming the profession, Spaniards usually say médico.
professions_médico: Médico is the standard word for a physician as a profession (soy médico). Doctor is used mainly as the title and form of address.
health_enfermera: Enfermera is a female nurse; a male nurse is enfermero.
professions_enfermero: Enfermero is a male nurse; a female nurse is enfermera.
professions_estudiante: Estudiante is the general word for a student at any level (el/la estudiante). Alumno frames someone as the pupil of a particular school or teacher.
school_alumno: Alumno is a pupil in relation to a school or teacher (los alumnos del colegio). The general word for a student is estudiante.
work_empresa: Empresa is the standard business word — una empresa de software. Compañía overlaps but leans toward fixed names (compañía aérea) and also means companionship.
shopping_compañía: Compañía means a company too — common in fixed names like compañía aérea — plus 'companionship'. The everyday business word is empresa.
hobbies_entrada: Entrada is an admission ticket — cinema, concerts, museums, matches (also 'entrance'). Transport tickets in Spain are billetes.
travel_billete: In Spain billete is a transport ticket — train, bus, plane — and also a banknote. Tickets that admit you to an event are entradas.
school_escuela: Escuela is the general word for school, including 'school of' (escuela de idiomas). Day-to-day, a children's school in Spain is usually el colegio.
school_colegio: Colegio (el cole) is what Spaniards usually call a children's school day-to-day. Escuela is the broader, more general term.
school_tarea: Tarea is a task or chore in general — and 'homework' mainly in Latin America. In Spain, school homework is usually los deberes.
school_deberes: Los deberes (always plural) is Spain's everyday word for homework. A single task or chore in general is una tarea.
school_diploma: Diploma is the certificate document itself, from any course. Spain's pre-university qualification is el bachillerato.
school_bachillerato: El bachillerato is Spain's two-year pre-university stage and its qualification — the closest thing to a 'high-school diploma', though not a paper certificate (that's un diploma).
school_aprobar: Aprobar is to pass an exam or course — and 'to approve'. Almost every other sense of 'pass' (pass by, pass time, hand over) is pasar.
core_pasar: Pasar covers most senses of 'to pass' — pass by, spend time, hand something over, happen. Passing an exam, though, is aprobar.
technology_computadora: Computadora is the Latin American word. In Spain the everyday word for computer is el ordenador.
technology_ordenador: Ordenador is Spain's word for computer. Most of Latin America says computadora.
numbers_primero: The ordinal 'first' — it drops -o before a masculine noun: el primer piso. The same word also sequences ideas: primero, …
question_primero: Here primero sequences ideas — 'first, …' at the start of an explanation. It's the same word as the ordinal number (el primer piso).
core_andar: Andar is Spain's everyday verb for walking or getting around — and machines 'run' with it too (mi reloj no anda). The deliberate physical act of walking is caminar.
actions_caminar: Caminar is to walk — the physical act itself, a walk or hike. In Spain, andar is often the everyday default (and covers machines 'running').
core_conocer: Conocer is to know people and places — to be familiar with (conozco Madrid, conozco a Ana). Facts and skills take saber.
core_saber: Saber is to know facts and how to do things (sé la respuesta, sé nadar). Being familiar with people and places takes conocer.
core_ser: Ser is 'to be' for identity and traits — who or what something is (soy médico, es alta). States, moods and locations take estar. Grammar Chapter 21 covers the full contrast.
core_estar: Estar is 'to be' for states, moods and locations — how or where something is (estoy cansado, está en casa). Identity and traits take ser. Grammar Chapter 21 covers the full contrast.
question_cuándo: With the accent, cuándo asks — in direct and indirect questions (¿cuándo vienes? / no sé cuándo). Without the accent it's the linking 'when'.
question_cuando: Without the accent, cuando links clauses — cuando llegues, llámame. Asking 'when?', directly or indirectly, takes the accent: cuándo.
